title: Making a wove mould
materials: softwood or hardwood strips (15mm × 6mm, kiln-dried, grain-straight)
fine mesh screen (40-60 mesh woven polyester or nylon)
copper or brass staples (for attaching the mesh)
sandpaper-240 for finishing the frame corners
sources: Wove mould construction documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923) — public domain. Western paper moulds from the 18th century onward; wove mesh (as opposed to laid wire) introduced c.1757 by James Whatman in England.
notes: The wove mould is the simpler of the two Western mould types (wove vs laid). The wove mesh produces a sheet with no wire-line pattern visible when held to light. Covers: selecting and preparing the frame timber (dry, straight-grained, sanded smooth), cutting the frame to the target sheet size plus mould frame width, joining the corners (simple butt joints with waterproof glue and staples), cutting and stretching the polyester mesh over the frame, stapling the mesh taut working from the centre of each side outward, trimming excess mesh. The deckle (the removable frame) is a separate matching frame, no mesh, described briefly. Difference from a laid mould (which requires laying chain wires over laid wires — more complex).
